We further show that the information flux can be increased even beyond the biologically embedded case by applying individually optimized biases to the core neurons, and that these biases can emerge from a simple self-organization principle. Our findings are relevant both for the functional interpretation of biological neural circuits and for the design of artificial recurrent systems such as reservoir computers.

</details>


<div id='stat.ML'></div>

# stat.ML [[Back]](#toc)

### [548] [AIS: Adaptive Importance Sampling for Quantized RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13907)
*Jiajun Zhou,Wei Shao,Lingchao Zheng,Yuwei Fan,Ngai Wong*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper addresses the rollout-training mismatch caused by using low-precision rollouts in reinforcement learning for large language models (LLMs) and proposes a correction framework called Adaptive Importance Sampling (AIS) to stabilize training. AIS preserves exploration benefits while mitigating destabilizing biases, achieving high performance and speed gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation behind this paper is to address the computational inefficiency and instability caused by low-precision rollout generation in reinforcement learning for LLMs, as these rollouts lead to a significant policy gradient bias and training collapse on reasoning benchmarks.

Method: The paper proposes Adaptive Importance Sampling (AIS), a framework that adjusts the correction on a per-batch basis using three diagnostics: weight reliability, divergence severity, and variance amplification. AIS interpolates between uncorrected and fully importance-weighted gradients, suppressing destabilizing effects while maintaining exploration.

Result: AIS was integrated into GRPO and tested on various benchmarks (mathematical reasoning and planning) using LLaDA-8B-Instruct and Qwen3-8B/Qwen3.5-9B. It achieved performance comparable to the BF16 baseline while retaining a rollout speedup of 1.5 to 2.76x.

Conclusion: AIS effectively balances the exploratory benefits and stability in RL for LLMs by mitigating biases from low-precision rollouts. This approach enables both high performance and computational efficiency, paving the way for more robust RL training in LLMs.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) for large language models (LLMs) is dominated by the cost of rollout generation, which has motivated the use of low-precision rollouts (e.g., FP8) paired with a BF16 trainer to improve throughput and reduce memory pressure. This introduces a rollout-training mismatch that biases the policy gradient and can cause training to collapse outright on reasoning benchmarks. We show that the mismatch is non-stationary and acts as a double-edged sword: early in training it provides a stochastic exploration bonus, exposing the gradient to trajectories the trainer would otherwise under-sample, but the same perturbation transitions into a destabilizing source of bias as the policy concentrates.
  To solve this, we propose Adaptive Importance Sampling (AIS), a correction framework that adjusts the strength of its intervention on a per-batch basis. AIS combines three real-time diagnostics, namely weight reliability, divergence severity, and variance amplification, into a single mixing coefficient that interpolates between the uncorrected and fully importance-weighted gradients, suppressing the destabilizing component of the mismatch while preserving its exploratory benefit. We integrate AIS into GRPO and evaluate it on the diffusion-based LLaDA-8B-Instruct and the autoregressive Qwen3-8B and Qwen3.5-9B across mathematical reasoning and planning benchmarks. AIS matches the BF16 baseline on most tasks while retaining the 1.5 to 2.76x rollout speedup of FP8.

</details>


### [549] [Covariance-aware sampling for Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13910)
*Andrea Schioppa,Tim Salimans*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper proposes a covariance-aware sampler that enhances the quality of pixel-space Diffusion Model (DM) sampling in few steps, using an efficient covariance modeling approach.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion models often perform poorly in the few-step sampling regime due to their reliance on predicted means without considering covariance. The authors aim to improve sample quality by explicitly modeling reverse-process covariance.

Method: The method utilizes Tweedie's formula for covariance estimation and applies a structured Fourier-space decomposition of the covariance matrix, integrated into DDIM, requiring minimal computational effort (one extra JVP per step).

Result: The proposed method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art second-order samplers like Heun, DPM-Solver++, and aDDIM in pixel-space DMs, with identical computational cost.

Conclusion: By addressing the limitations of current few-step samplers, this approach improves sample quality without increasing the computational burden, making it a significant advancement in DM technology.

Abstract: We present a covariance-aware sampler that improves the quality of pixel-space Diffusion Model (DM) sampling in the few-step regime. We hypothesize that in the few-step regime samplers fail because they rely solely on the predicted mean of the reverse distribution, while our solution explicitly models the reverse-process covariance. Our method combines Tweedie's formula to estimate the covariance with an efficient, structured Fourier-space decomposition of the covariance matrix. Implemented as an extension of DDIM, our method requires only a minimal overhead: one extra Jacobian-Vector Product (JVP) per step. We demonstrate that for pixel-based DMs, our method consistently produces superior samples compared to state-of-the-art second order samplers (Heun, DPM-Solver++) and the recent aDDIM sampler, at an identical number of function evaluations (NFE).

</details>


### [550] [A Survey on Data-Dependent Worst-Case Generalization Bounds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13913)
*Hubert Leroux,Jean Marcus,Julien Roger*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: Overparameterized deep neural networks generalize well, challenging classical learning theory, and this paper surveys advances in refining generalization bounds through data-dependent hypothesis sets and geometric/topological analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical learning theory falls short in explaining the generalization power of overparameterized deep neural networks, necessitating new frameworks for understanding their behavior.

Method: The paper combines three approaches: (1) PAC-Bayesian theory for data-dependent hypothesis sets, (2) complexity refinements using geometric and topological factors like fractal dimensions, and (3) simplifying bounds with stability assumptions.

Result: A unified template inequality and head-to-head comparison of generalization bounds that incorporate geometric and stability-centric descriptors of optimization behavior is presented.

Conclusion: The surveyed frameworks significantly advance understanding and predicting generalization in deep learning, particularly for heavily overparameterized models.

Abstract: Deep neural networks generalize well despite being heavily overparameterized, in apparent contradiction with classical learning theory based on uniform convergence over fixed hypothesis spaces. Uniform bounds over the entire parameter space are vacuous in this regime, and recent work has shown that non-vacuous guarantees can be recovered by restricting attention to the part of parameter space that the algorithm actually visits. This survey paper organizes this line of work around three steps: extending PAC-Bayesian theory to random, data-dependent hypothesis sets (arXiv:2404.17442); refining the complexity term with geometric and topological descriptors of the optimization trajectory, including fractal dimensions, alpha-weighted lifetime sums, and positive magnitude (arXiv:2006.09313, arXiv:2302.02766, arXiv:2407.08723); and replacing the resulting information-theoretic terms by stability assumptions (arXiv:2507.06775). We unify these contributions around a single template inequality and a head-to-head comparison of the resulting bounds.

</details>


### [551] [Multi-Scale Dequant: Eliminating Dequantization Bottleneck via Activation Decomposition for Efficient LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13915)
*Lingchao Zheng,Yuwei Fan,Jun Li,Chengqiu Hu,Qichen Liao,Junyi Fan,Rui Shi,Fangzheng Miao*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: Quantization improves efficiency in large language model inference but faces bottlenecks due to dequantization steps. This paper introduces Multi-Scale Dequant (MSD), a framework eliminating weight dequantization using a multi-scale approximation approach.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern AI accelerators experience critical inefficiencies during dequantization operations, leading to underutilization of tensor cores due to the computational bottleneck of converting low-bit weights back to high-precision formats.

Method: MSD introduces a multi-scale decomposition method, which especially focuses on using decomposed high-precision activations to multiply directly with quantized weights. Two specific formats, INT8 and MXFP4, are explored with error bounds derived for computational efficiency.

Result: MSD achieves computational gains by avoiding dequantization bottlenecks, reduces HBM traffic by up to 2.5 times, and preserves baseline accuracy while offering lower L2 error in many situations.

Conclusion: MSD showcases potential in revolutionizing quantization techniques by efficiently handling matrix multiplication cycles, resolving historic inefficiencies in AI accelerators, and maintaining computational success.

Abstract: Quantization is essential for efficient large language model (LLM) inference, yet the dequantization step-converting low-bit weights back to high-precision for matrix multiplication has become a critical bottleneck on modern AI accelerators. On architectures with decoupled compute units (e.g., Ascend NPUs), dequantization operations can consume more cycles than the matrix multiplication itself, leaving the high-throughput tensor cores underutilized. This paper presents Multi-Scale Dequant (MSD), a quantization framework that removes weight/KV dequantization from the GEMM critical path. Instead of lifting low-bit weights to BF16 precision, MSD decomposes high-precision BF16 activations into multiple low-precision components, each of which can be multiplied directly with quantized weights via native hardware-accelerated GEMM. This approach shifts the computational paradigm from precision conversion to multi-scale approximation, avoiding INT8-to-BF16 weight conversion before GEMM. We instantiate MSD for two weight formats and derive tight error bounds for each. For INT8 weights (W4A16), two-pass INT8 decomposition achieves near 16 effective bits. For MXFP4 weights (W4A16), two-pass MXFP4 decomposition yields near 6.6 effective bits with error bound 1/64 per block surpassing single-pass MXFP8(5.24 bits) while maintaining the same effective GEMM compute time. We further derive closed-form latency and HBM traffic models showing that MSD avoids the Vector-Cube pipeline stall caused by dequantization and reduces KV cache HBM traffic by up to 2.5 times in attention. Numerical simulations on matrix multiplication and Flash Attention kernels confirm that MSD does not degrade accuracy compared to dequantization baselines, and in many settings achieves lower L2 error.

</details>


### [552] [A Regret Perspective on Online Multiple Testing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13916)
*Qingyang Hao,Kongchang Zhou,Fang Kong,Hongxin Wei*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces Weighted Regret as a unifying evaluation metric for Online Multiple Testing (OMT) and proposes the Decoupled-OMT (DOMT) meta-wrapper to address challenges in existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of traditional metrics used in Online Multiple Testing (OMT), which evaluate False Discovery Rate (FDR) and statistical power separately, neglecting asymmetric costs of errors in automated systems.

Method: The authors propose Decoupled-OMT (DOMT), a meta-wrapper incorporating random perturbations to overcome threshold depletion in deterministic baselines, while preserving asymptotic safety and minimizing finite-sample errors.

Result: DOMT achieves order-optimal sublinear Weighted Regret reduction, avoids false negatives, and mitigates threshold depletion, particularly in bursty or cold-start scenarios. Experiments confirm its superiority.

Conclusion: DOMT effectively addresses threshold depletion, achieves empirical Weighted Regret reduction, and provides a practical path to navigating the Pareto frontier in non-stationary OMT settings.

Abstract: Online Multiple Testing (OMT), a fundamental pillar of sequential statistical inference, traditionally evaluates the False Discovery Rate (FDR) and statistical power in isolation, obscuring the highly asymmetric costs of false positives and false negatives in modern automated pipelines. To unify this evaluation, we introduce $\textit{Weighted Regret}$. Under this metric, we prove the $\textit{Duality of Regret Conservation}$: purely deterministic procedures ensuring strict FDR control inevitably incur an $Ω(T)$ linear regret penalty, as threshold depletion during signal-sparse cold starts forces massive false negatives. Tailored for exogenous testing streams, we propose Decoupled-OMT (DOMT) as a baseline-agnostic meta-wrapper. By incorporating a history-decoupled, strictly non-negative random perturbation, DOMT rescues purely deterministic baselines from severe threshold depletion. Crucially, it preserves exact asymptotic safety in stationary environments and rigorously bounds finite-sample error inflation during cold-starts. Guaranteeing zero additional false negatives, it yields an order-optimal $Ω(\sqrt{T})$ regret reduction in bursty environments, with a derived ``Cold-Start Tax'' characterizing the exact phase transition of algorithmic superiority. Experiments validate that DOMT consistently curtails empirical weighted regret, achieving an order-optimal sublinear mitigation of threshold depletion to navigate the non-stationary Pareto frontier.

</details>


### [553] [Pause and Reflect: Conformal Aggregation for Chain-of-Thought Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14098)
*Yu Gu,Zijun Yu,Vahid Partovi Nia,Masoud Asgharian*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper proposes a method for improving Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning by replacing majority voting with a weighted score aggregation method and introducing conformal risk control, resulting in finite-sample guarantees on confident-error rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the challenge of aggregation uncertainty in CoT reasoning, where confidently incorrect answers are more costly than abstentions, thereby improving the reliability and accuracy of reasoning paths.

Method: The proposed method introduces weighted score aggregation for reasoning paths and employs conformal risk control to calibrate an abstention rule. This results in finite-sample guarantees on the confident-error rate, and the approach operates completely at inference-time without retraining.

Result: The method significantly improves selective accuracy across four benchmarks, achieving rates like 90.1% on GSM8K with less than 5% abstention, outperforming majority-voting baselines.

Conclusion: The new approach effectively enhances the reliability of CoT reasoning by providing a systematic means to manage confident-errors and improve accuracy through calibrated abstentions, all without necessitating model retraining.

Abstract: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning with self-consistency improves performance by aggregating multiple sampled reasoning paths. In this setting, correctness is no longer tied to a single reasoning trace but to the aggregation rule over a pool of candidate paths, making aggregation uncertainty the central challenge. This issue is critical where confidently incorrect answers are far more costly than abstentions. We introduce a conformal procedure for CoT reasoning that directly addresses aggregation uncertainty. Our approach replaces majority voting with weighted score aggregation over reasoning paths and calibrates an abstention rule using conformal risk control. This approach leads to finite-sample guarantees on the confident-error rate--the probability that the system answers and is wrong. We further identify score separability as the key condition under which abstention provably improves selective accuracy, and derive closed-form expressions that predict accuracy gains from calibration data alone. The method is fully inference-time, and requires no retraining. Across four benchmarks, four open-source models, and three score classes, realized confident-error rates are consistent with the prescribed targets up to calibration-split and test-set variability. Our method achieves $90.1\%$ selective accuracy on GSM8K by abstaining on less than $5\%$ of problems, compared with $82\%$ accuracy under majority-voting baseline.

</details>


### [554] [To discretize continually: Mean shift interacting particle systems for Bayesian inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14142)
*Ayoub Belhadji,Daniel Sharp,Youssef M. Marzouk*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper proposes methods for approximating integrals against unnormalized densities using weighted samples created through particle systems minimizing the maximum mean discrepancy (MMD).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of estimating expectations in Bayesian inference and related domains when only unnormalized densities are available.

Method: The authors develop interacting particle systems based on MMD minimization, building upon mean shift algorithms and optimal quantization techniques, and implement them in gradient-free and gradient-informed versions.

Result: The proposed techniques demonstrate fast convergence, handle multi-modality and anisotropy well, avoid mode collapse, and perform effectively in high-dimensional problems.

Conclusion: The particle systems are broadly applicable and show superior performance across diverse sampling problems, showcasing their potential for Bayesian inference and related tasks.

Abstract: Integration against a probability distribution given its unnormalized density is a central task in Bayesian inference and other fields. We introduce new methods for approximating such expectations with a small set of weighted samples -- i.e., a quadrature rule -- constructed via an interacting particle system that minimizes maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) to the target distribution. These methods extend the classical mean shift algorithm, as well as recent algorithms for optimal quantization of empirical distributions, to the case of continuous distributions. Crucially, our approach creates dynamics for MMD minimization that are invariant to the unknown normalizing constant; they also admit both gradient-free and gradient-informed implementations. The resulting mean shift interacting particle systems converge quickly, capture anisotropy and multi-modality, avoid mode collapse, and scale to high dimensions. We demonstrate their performance on a wide range of benchmark sampling problems, including multi-modal mixtures, Bayesian hierarchical models, PDE-constrained inverse problems, and beyond.

</details>


### [555] [On the Burden of Achieving Fairness in Conformal Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14260)
*Ziang Gao,Pengqi Liu,Archer Yi Yang,Mouloud Belbahri,Jesse C. Cresswell,Masoud Asgharian*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper studies fairness and calibration trade-offs in conformal prediction, highlighting challenges in pooled calibration and group fairness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address fairness concerns in conformal prediction, where pooled calibration may lead to distortions in group-wise coverage due to cross-group heterogeneity.

Method: The study derives theoretical results on cross-group heterogeneity and coverage, analyzes tensions between fairness definitions (Equalized Coverage and Equalized Set Size), and conducts experiments with both synthetic and real data.

Result: Theoretical and empirical findings reveal inherent trade-offs in conformal prediction between coverage accuracy and fairness across groups, driven by calibration choices.

Conclusion: Calibration in conformal prediction cannot entirely eliminate cross-group heterogeneity, but it determines whether fairness distortions manifest in coverage or set size, offering insights for analysis and policy decisions.

Abstract: Conformal prediction is often calibrated with a single pooled threshold, but this can hide cross-group heterogeneity in score distributions and distort group-wise coverage. We study this phenomenon through the population score distributions underlying split conformal calibration. First, we derive a conservation law and lower bound showing that pooled calibration incurs irreducible group-wise coverage distortion at a scale set by cross-group quantile heterogeneity. Second, we demonstrate that the two leading fairness definitions for conformal prediction, Equalized Coverage and Equalized Set Size, are fundamentally in tension. Third, we quantify the cost of moving between policies which treat groups separately or pool them. Experiments on synthetic and real data confirm the same bidirectional trade-off after finite-sample calibration. Our results show that, for the policy families studied here, calibration choice does not remove cross-group heterogeneity; it determines whether the resulting distortion appears in the coverage or size dimension, providing a principled lens for analyzing fairness-oriented calibration choices in practice.

</details>


### [556] [Training-Free Generative Sampling via Moment-Matched Score Smoothing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14276)
*Zhenyu Yao,Daniel Paulin*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces MM-SOLD, a training-free sampling approach, which shows competitive performance to neural diffusion methods, without expensive model training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the high computational costs of training neural diffusion models and improve sampling efficiency, while preserving image fidelity and diversity.

Method: The authors propose MM-SOLD, a moment-matched score-smoothed interacting particle sampler that enforces target moments over the sampling process. It avoids training by ensuring the empirical moments of the samples align with the training data.

Result: Experiments on 2D distributions and latent-space image generation demonstrate that MM-SOLD offers fast, efficient, CPU-compatible sampling, achieving fidelity and diversity comparable to trained diffusion models.

Conclusion: MM-SOLD achieves competitive results with neural diffusion models without the need for computationally expensive training, providing an efficient alternative for tasks requiring high-quality sampling.

Abstract: Diffusion models generate samples by denoising along the score of a perturbed target distribution. In practice, one trains a neural diffusion model, which is computationally expensive. Recent work suggests that score matching implicitly smooths the empirical score, and that this smoothing bias promotes generalization by capturing low-dimensional data geometry. We propose moment-matched score-smoothed overdamped Langevin dynamics (MM-SOLD), a training-free interacting particle sampler that enforces the target moments throughout the sampling trajectory. We prove that, in the large-particle limit, the empirical particle density converges to a deterministic limit whose one-particle stationary marginal is a Gibbs--Boltzmann density obtained by exponentially tilting a naive score-smoothed diffusion target. The mean and covariance of this distribution agree with the empirical moments of the training data. Experiments on 2D distributions and latent-space image generation show that MM-SOLD enables fast, robust, training-free sampling on CPUs, with sample fidelity and diversity competitive with neural diffusion baselines.

</details>


### [557] [Large Dimensional Kernel Ridge Regression: Extending to Product Kernels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14524)
*Yang Zhou,Yicheng Li,Yuqian Cheng,Qian Lin*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper extends findings on saturation effects and multiple descent behavior in Kernel Ridge Regression (KRR) to a broader class of large-dimensional kernels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to determine whether the phenomena observed in KRR, such as saturation effects and multiple descent behavior, apply beyond inner product kernels on spheres, which are studied under restrictive settings.

Method: The authors establish a new family of large-dimensional kernels and analyze the convergence rates of generalization errors associated with these kernels.

Result: The study recovers known phenomena like minimax optimality for specific conditions, saturation effects, periodic plateau behavior in convergence rates, and multiple-descent phenomena tied to sample size.

Conclusion: This work generalizes findings from KRR to a more diverse set of kernels, shedding light on convergence and error dynamics in high-dimensional settings.

Abstract: Recent studies have reported $\textit{saturation effects}$ and $\textit{multiple descent behavior}$ in large dimensional kernel ridge regression (KRR). However, these findings are predominantly derived under restrictive settings, such as inner product kernels on sphere or strong eigenfunction assumptions like hypercontractivity. Whether such behaviors hold for other kernels remains an open question. In this paper, we establish a broad, new family of large dimensional kernels and derive the corresponding convergence rates of the generalization error. As a result, we recover key phenomena previously associated with inner product kernels on sphere, including: $i)$ the $\textit{minimax optimality}$ when the source condition $s\le 1$; $ii)$ the $\textit{saturation effect}$ when $s>1$; $iii)$ a $\textit{periodic plateau phenomenon}$ in the convergence rate and a $\textit {multiple-descent behavior}$ with respect to the sample size $n$.

</details>


### [558] [Scaling Laws from Sequential Feature Recovery: A Solvable Hierarchical Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14567)
*Arie Wortsman-Zurich,Hugo Tabanelli,Yatin Dandi,Florent Krzakala,Bruno Loureiro*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper examines how scaling laws emerge from feature learning in multi-layer networks, proposing a spectral algorithm adapted to hierarchical compositional structures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding how scaling laws are formed in feature learning can enhance the understanding and design of neural networks.

Method: A layer-wise spectral algorithm was used, relying on random matrix methods and resolvent-based perturbation arguments to analyze hierarchical compositional features.

Result: Sharp feature-wise recovery thresholds were proven, showcasing a power-law decay in prediction error, supported by numerical experiments verifying sequential recovery and separation from kernel baselines.

Conclusion: Smooth scaling laws arise from sharp feature-learning transitions in hierarchical models, with implications for neural network design and scalability.

Abstract: We propose a simple mechanism by which scaling laws emerge from feature learning in multi-layer networks. We study a high-dimensional hierarchical target that is a globally high-degree function, but that can be represented by a combination of latent compositional features whose weights decrease as a power law. We show that a layer-wise spectral algorithm adapted to this compositional structure achieves improved scaling relative to shallow, non-adaptive methods, and recovers the latent directions sequentially: strong features become detectable at small sample sizes, while weaker features require more data. We prove sharp feature-wise recovery thresholds and show that aggregating these transitions yields an explicit power-law decay of the prediction error. Technically, the analysis relies on random matrix methods and a resolvent-based perturbation argument, which gives matching upper and lower bounds for individual eigenvector recovery beyond what standard gap-based perturbation bounds provide. Numerical experiments confirm the predicted sequential recovery, finite-size smoothing of the thresholds, and separation from non-hierarchical kernel baselines. Together, these results show how smooth scaling laws can emerge from a cascade of sharp feature-learning transitions.

</details>


### [559] [K-Models: a Flexible and Interpretable Method for Ordinal Clustering with Application to Antigen-Antibody Interaction Profiles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14828)
*Giulia Patanè,Alessandra Menafoglio,Alexander Krauth,Peter Fechner,Luca Dede',Bianca Maria Colosimo,Federica Nicolussi*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: K-Models is a new clustering framework that integrates ordinal constraints for improved interpretability and structural insights, assessed on synthetic and real-world datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional clustering methods often sacrifice interpretability for partitioning accuracy, which becomes problematic when data exhibit underlying structures and ordinal relationships among clusters.

Method: K-Models introduces ordinal constraints and estimates key factors of the random process generating functional data. It tests this framework on both simulated data and real-world ROI curves from biomolecular interaction monitoring.

Result: K-Models achieves enhanced interpretability while preserving performance comparable with state-of-the-art clustering methods.

Conclusion: The method provides a robust tool for analyzing functional data with ordinal relationships, proving its practicality across varied datasets and applications.

Abstract: Existing clustering methods for functional data often prioritize partitioning accuracy over interpretability, making it challenging to extract meaningful insights when the data-generating process follows a specific underlying structure and an ordinal relationship among clusters is suspected. This work introduces K-Models, a novel framework that integrates ordinal constraints and estimates key underlying elements of the random process generating the observed functional profiles, improving both interpretability and structure identification. The proposed method is evaluated through simulations and real-world applications. In particular, it is tested on Region of Interest (ROI) curves, which represent reaction profiles from a reflectometric sensor monitoring biomolecular interactions, such as antigen-antibody binding. These curves represent changes in reflected light intensity over time at multiple measurement spots with immobilized antigens during analyte exposure, capturing the binding dynamics of the system. The goal is to identify intrinsic signal patterns solely from the observed dynamics, making this dataset an ideal benchmark for assessing the added interpretability of the proposed approach. By incorporating structural assumptions into the clustering process, K-Models enhances interpretability while maintaining performance comparable to state-of-the-art techniques, providing a valuable tool for analyzing functional data with an underlying ordinal structure.

</details>


### [560] [Average Gradient Outer Product in kernel regression provably recovers the central subspace for multi-index models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15082)
*Libin Zhu,Damek Davis,Dmitriy Drusvyatskiy,Maryam Fazel*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper investigates how a learned predictor can identify low-dimensional structures in data using fewer samples than needed for accurate predictions. This is achieved through analyzing a kernel ridge regression (KRR)-based method.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to understand how predictors can uncover useful low-dimensional structures in data, enabling sample-efficient learning and feature extraction in high-dimensional scenarios.

Method: The authors propose fitting kernel ridge regression (KRR) to the data and applying the Average Gradient Outer Product (AGOP) to recover the low-dimensional subspace that captures the target function.

Result: Under specified assumptions, the algorithm recovers the central subspace efficiently, requiring significantly fewer samples (n ~ d^(p+δ), δ∈(0,1)) compared to what is needed for accurate prediction.

Conclusion: The study highlights a separation between prediction and representation, explaining the sample efficiency of iterative kernel methods like Recursive Feature Machines (RFM).

Abstract: We study a prototypical situation when a learned predictor can discover useful low-dimensional structure in data, while using fewer samples than are needed for accurate prediction. Specifically, we consider the problem of recovering a multi-index polynomial $f^*(x)=h(Ux)$, with $U\in\mathbb{R}^{r\times d}$ and $r\ll d$, from finitely many data/label pairs. Importantly, the target function depends on input $x$ only through the projection onto an unknown $r$-dimensional central subspace. The algorithm we analyze is appealingly simple: fit kernel ridge regression (KRR) to the data and compute the Average Gradient Outer Product (AGOP) from the fitted predictor. Our main results show that under reasonable assumptions the top $r$-dimensional eigenspace of AGOP provably recovers the central subspace, even in regimes when the prediction error remains large. Specifically, if the target function $f^*$ has degree $p^*$, it is known that $n\asymp d^{p^*}$ samples are necessary for KRR to achieve accurate prediction. In contrast, we show that if a low degree $p$ component of $f^*$ already carries all relevant directions for prediction, subspace recovery occurs in the much lower sample regime $n\asymp d^{p+δ}$ for any $δ\in(0,1)$. Our results thus demonstrate a separation between prediction and representation, and provide an explanation for why iterative kernel methods such as Recursive Feature Machines (RFM) can be sample-efficient in practice.

</details>


### [561] [From Data to Action: Accelerating Refinery Optimization with AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15085)
*Dániel Pfeifer,Ábrahám Papp,Tibor Bernáth,Tamás Zoltán Varga,Márk Czifra,Botond Szilágyi,Edith Alice Kovács*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper combines Linear Programming (LP) with modified Anomaly Detection methods to analyze refinery optimization data, identifying business opportunities and data errors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large petrochemical companies need better tools to interpret and trust the results of LP models due to inherent simplifications and data supply errors.

Method: The study applies a transformed ECOD methodology and proposes methods for selecting informative data pairs. Paired with 2D Anomaly Detection algorithms, it improves analysis of high-dimensional refinery data.

Result: The approach identified several business opportunities and data errors in a refinery's scheduling and planning processes.

Conclusion: Machine learning can enhance LP results by analyzing high-dimensional data, offering actionable insights and improving decision-making in refinery optimization.

Abstract: Nowadays refinery optimization utilizes sheer amounts of data, which can be handled with modern Linear Programming (LP) software, but the interpreting and applying the results remains challenging. Large petrochemical companies use massive models, with hundreds of thousands of input matrix elements. The LP solution is mathematically correct, but simplifications are made in the model, and data supply errors may occur. Therefore, further insight is needed to trust the results. The LP solver does not have a memory, so additional understanding could be gained by analyzing historical data and comparing it to the current plan. As such, machine learning approaches were suggested to support decision making based on the LP solution. Among these, Anomaly Detection tools are proposed to be used in tandem with the LP output. A transformed version of the popular ECOD methodology is applied. New methods are proposed to handle high-dimensional data: choosing the most informative pairs. Then, this is used alongside two 2D Anomaly Detection algorithms, revealing several business opportunities and data supply errors in the MOL refinery scheduling and planning architecture.

</details>


### [562] [Logging Policy Design for Off-Policy Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15108)
*Connor Douglas,Joel Persson,Foster Provost*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper addresses designing logging policies to minimize off-policy evaluation (OPE) errors for target policies, introducing a framework and deriving optimal designs for various knowledge scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the accuracy of OPE by addressing how the choice of logging policies influences the estimation of target treatment policies, especially in high-stakes environments.

Method: It characterizes a tradeoff in logging policy design between reward concentration and action coverage, proposing a unifying framework for designing optimal logging policies under different knowledge scenarios (known, unknown, and partially known distributions).

Result: The study derives theoretically optimal logging policies under various conditions and provides guidance for firms to improve their OPE practices, even under operational constraints.

Conclusion: Optimal design of logging policies significantly enhances OPE accuracy. The paper offers theoretical insights and practical principles for firms to effectively select or optimize logging policies when data is collected for OPE.

Abstract: Off-policy evaluation (OPE) estimates the value of a target treatment policy (e.g., a recommender system) using data collected by a different logging policy. It enables high-stakes experimentation without live deployment, yet in practice accuracy depends heavily on the logging policy used to collect data for computing the estimate. We study how to design logging policies that minimize OPE error for given target policies. We characterize a fundamental reward-coverage tradeoff: concentrating probability mass on high-reward actions reduces variance but risks missing signal on actions the target policy may take. We propose a unifying framework for logging policy design and derive optimal policies in canonical informational regimes where the target policy and reward distribution are (i) known, (ii) unknown, and (iii) partially known through priors or noisy estimates at logging time. Our results provide actionable guidance for firms choosing among multiple candidate recommendation systems. We demonstrate the importance of treatment selection when gathering data for OPE, and describe theoretically optimal approaches when this is a firm's primary objective. We also distill practical design principles for selecting logging policies when operational constraints prevent implementing the theoretical optimum.

</details>


### [563] [RoSHAP: A Distributional Framework and Robust Metric for Stable Feature Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15154)
*Lanxin Xiang,Liang Shi,Youhui Ye,Boyu Jiang,Dawei Zhou,Feng Guo*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper tackles the issue of unstable feature attribution in machine learning models and introduces RoSHAP, a robust SHAP-based metric that stabilizes feature rankings using statistical methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Feature attribution measures often show variability due to randomness in training/testing splits or model-fitting, and reliable insights from unstable attributions are difficult to achieve.

Method: The authors propose statistical modeling of feature attribution distribution using bootstrap resampling and kernel density estimation, introducing RoSHAP to reward features that are active, strong, and stable.

Result: Simulation and real-data experiments demonstrated that RoSHAP outperforms standard methods in detecting signal features, enabling models to achieve comparable predictive performance using fewer predictors.

Conclusion: RoSHAP increases stability and interpretability in machine learning models, promoting reliable data-driven decisions while reducing feature count without sacrificing predictive accuracy.

Abstract: Feature attribution analysis is critical for interpreting machine learning models and supporting reliable data-driven decisions. However, feature attribution measures often exhibit stochastic variation: different train--test splits, random seeds, or model-fitting procedures can produce substantially different attribution values and feature rankings. This paper proposes a framework for incorporating stochastic nature of feature attribution and a robust attribution metric, RoSHAP, for stable feature ranking based on the SHAP metric. The proposed framework models the distribution of feature attribution scores and estimates it through bootstrap resampling and kernel density estimation. We show that, under mild regularity conditions, the aggregated feature attribution score is asymptotically Gaussian, which greatly reduces the computational cost of distribution estimation. The RoSHAP summarizes the distribution of SHAP into a robust feature-ranking criterion that simultaneously rewards features that are active, strong, and stable. Through simulations and real-data experiments, the proposed framework and RoSHAP outperform standard single-run attribution measures in identifying signal features. In addition, models built using RoSHAP-selected features achieve predictive performance comparable to full-feature models while using substantially fewer predictors. The proposed RoSHAP approach improves the stability and interpretability of machine learning models, enabling reliable and consistent insights for analysis.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.QM'></div>

# q-bio.QM [[Back]](#toc)

### [564] [Attention-Based Multimodal Survival Prediction with Cross-Modal Bilinear Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13897)
*Hassan Keshvarikhojasteh,Josien P. W. Pluim,Mitko Veta*

Main category: q-bio.QM

TL;DR: This paper proposes a deep learning framework that integrates histology, RNA-seq, and clinical data for patient survival prediction, using a bilinear fusion design for improved interpretability and performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve survival prediction accuracy in medical settings by integrating multimodal data (histology, RNA-seq, and clinical information) while maintaining parameter efficiency and interpretability.

Method: The proposed method uses ABMIL for slide representation, feedforward encoders for RNA and clinical data, and low-rank bilinear fusion to combine data. Risk scores are calibrated with a Kaplan-Meier-based nonparametric approach.

Result: The model improves predictive performance over baselines and generalizes well on hidden evaluation cohorts in experiments on the CHIMERA challenge dataset.

Conclusion: The framework is effective for multimodal survival prediction, offering both enhanced performance and interpretability, and represents a promising approach for such tasks in HR-NMIBC.

Abstract: We propose a novel multimodal deep learning framework for patient-level survival prediction, which integrates whole-slide histology features, RNA-seq expression profiles, and clinical variables. Our architecture combines an ABMIL module~\cite{ilse2018attention} for slide-level representation with feedforward encoders for RNA and clinical data. These embeddings are then integrated through low-rank bilinear cross-modal fusion~\cite{liu2018efficient} to model conditional interactions across modalities while controlling parameter growth. The model outputs continuous risk scores that are subsequently mapped to survival times using a nonparametric calibration procedure based on the Kaplan--Meier estimator~\cite{kaplan1958nonparametric}. By decomposing multimodal reasoning into independent pairwise interactions, the proposed fusion design promotes structural interpretability and parameter efficiency compared with full tensor and hierarchical fusion strategies. Experiments on the CHIMERA challenge dataset demonstrate improved predictive performance over concatenation-based baselines and competitive generalization on hidden evaluation cohorts. These results indicate that the proposed framework is a promising approach for multimodal survival prediction in HR-NMIBC. The implementation is publicly available at https://github.com/hassancpu/ChimeraChallenge2025_Task_3.

</details>


<div id='cs.DS'></div>

# cs.DS [[Back]](#toc)

### [565] [Stochastic Matching via Local Sparsification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14195)
*Sara Ahmadian,Edith Cohen,Mohammad Roghani*

Main category: cs.DS

TL;DR: The paper introduces a two-stage local sparsification framework for online stochastic matching, allowing arriving requests to prune their potential matches before global optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern decentralized systems like ride-hailing and cloud computing often face bandwidth limitations, necessitating new strategies for balancing local constraints with global optimization.

Method: Propose a local selection strategy using a parametrized fractional solution of expected instances and quantify the approximation ratio in relation to the solution's spread.

Result: The approach theoretically preserves the expected maximum matching size under sufficient spread and empirically outperforms traditional baselines on real-world and synthetic datasets.

Conclusion: The proposed framework achieves near-optimal global matchings despite strict local budget constraints, showcasing its potential for practical use cases in bandwidth-limited systems.

Abstract: The classic online stochastic matching problem typically requires immediate and irrevocable matching decisions. However, in many modern decentralized systems such as real-time ride-hailing and distributed cloud computing, the primary bottleneck is often local communication bandwidth rather than the timing of the match itself. We formalize this challenge by introducing a two-stage local sparsification framework. In this setting, arriving requests must prune their realized compatibility sets to a strict budget of $k$ edges before a central coordinator optimizes the global matching. This creates a "middle ground" between local information constraints and global optimization utility.
  We propose a local selection strategy, parametrized by a fractional solution of the expected instance. Theoretically, we quantify the approximation ratio as a function of the solution's {\em spread}. We prove that under sufficient spread, our sparsifier globally preserves the expected size of the maximum matching. Empirically, we demonstrate the robustness of our approach using the New York City ride-hailing datasets and adversarial synthetic benchmarks. Our results show that near-optimal global matching is achievable even with highly constrained local budgets, significantly outperforming standard online baselines.

</details>


<div id='cs.SD'></div>

# cs.SD [[Back]](#toc)

### [566] [Persian MusicGen: A Large-Scale Dataset and Culturally-Aware Generative Model for Persian Music](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14765)
*Mohammad Hossein Sameti,Diba Hadi Esfangereh,Sepehr Harfi Moridani,Leili Javidpour,Mahdieh Soleymani Baghshah*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper presents the first large-scale dataset of Persian music, fine-tunes the MusicGen model on it, and demonstrates improved alignment with Persian stylistic conventions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Persian music remains underrepresented in AI music generation research. Most models are trained on Western music, failing to capture Persian tonalities, modal systems, and rhythmic structures.

Method: A large-scale dataset of Persian songs was curated, and the state-of-the-art generative model MusicGen was fine-tuned on this dataset. The evaluation included subjective and objective metrics, focusing on semantic alignment with style tags.

Result: The fine-tuned MusicGen model produces music compositions that more closely align with Persian stylistic conventions, as validated by both subjective and objective assessments.

Conclusion: This work introduces a valuable new dataset for research and demonstrates that generative music models can adapt effectively to culturally unique and underrepresented musical styles.

Abstract: Persian music, with its unique tonalities, modal systems (Dastgah), and rhythmic structures, presents significant challenges for music generation models trained primarily on Western music. We address this gap by curating the first large-scale dataset of Persian songs, comprising over 900 hours high-quality audio samples across diverse sub-genres, including pop, traditional, and contemporary styles. This dataset captures the rich melodic and cultural diversity of Persian music and serves as the foundation for fine-tuning MusicGen, a state-of-the-art generative music model. We adapt MusicGen to this domain and evaluate its performance by utilizing subjective and objective metrics. To assess the semantic alignment between generated music and intended style tags, we report the proportion of relevant tags accurately reflected in the generated outputs. Our results demonstrate that the fine-tuned model produces compositions that more align with Persian stylistic conventions. This work introduces a new resource for generative music research and illustrates the adaptability of music generation models to underrepresented cultural and linguistic contexts.

</details>


### [567] [Masked Autoencoders with Limited Data: Does It Work? A Fine-Grained Bioacoustics Case Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14031)
*Wuao Liu,Mustafa Chasmai,Subhransu Maji,Grant Van Horn*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper examines masked autoencoders (MAE)-based pretraining for bioacoustic species classification using iNatSounds data, demonstrating that diverse audio pretraining outperforms domain-specific or selectively filtered data in modest-sized datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study addresses the challenge of training bioacoustic models with weakly annotated data, such as iNaturalist collections, which complicates supervised learning due to minimal labeling complexity.

Method: The authors systematically study the effects of MAE pretraining by analyzing impacts from data scale, domain specificity, curation methods, and transfer strategies on bioacoustic species classification.

Result: The findings reveal that pretraining on diverse general audio achieves superior performance compared to domain-specific or filtered datasets, and that larger pretraining data scale is more impactful than objective design in this context.

Conclusion: In fine-grained bioacoustic settings with moderate data sizes, the scale of pretraining dominates over data curation or domain adaptation strategies, offering practical insights into model selection for limited supervision environments.

Abstract: Bioacoustic recognition requires fine-grained acoustic understanding to distinguish similar-sounding species. However, many large-scale data repositories such as iNaturalist are weakly annotated, often with only a single positive species label per recording, making supervised learning particularly challenging. Inspired by advances in computer vision, recent approaches have shifted toward self-supervised learning to capture the underlying structure of audio without relying on exhaustive annotations. In particular, masked autoencoders (MAE) have shown strong transferability on massive audio corpora, yet their effectiveness in more modest bioacoustic settings remains underexplored. In this work, we conduct a systematic study of MAE pretraining for species classification on iNatSounds, analyzing the impacts of pretraining data scale, domain specificity, data curation, and transfer strategies. Consistent with prior work, we find that models pretrained on diverse general audio data achieve the best transfer performance on iNatSounds. Contrary to observations from large-scale audio benchmarks, we find that (1) additional masked reconstruction pretraining on domain-specific data provides limited benefits and may even degrade performance relative to off-the-shelf models, and (2) selective data filtering offers a negligible advantage when the overall data scale is limited. Our results indicate that, in moderate-sized fine-grained bioacoustic settings, pretraining scale dominates objective design. These findings further clarify when MAE-based pretraining is effective and provide practical guidance for model selection under limited supervision.

</details>


### [568] [Break-the-Beat! Controllable MIDI-to-Drum Audio Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14555)
*Shuyang Cui,Zhi Zhong,Qiyu Wu,Zachary Novack,Woosung Choi,Keisuke Toyama,Kin Wai Cheuk,Junghyun Koo,Yukara Ikemiya,Christian Simon,Chihiro Nagashima,Shusuke Takahashi*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: Break-the-Beat! introduces a method to generate drum loop audio by synthesizing drum MIDI in reference to an input audio timbre, offering enhanced creative control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of specific control in generative models for drum loop synthesis and the gap in symbolic-to-audio research for polyphonic, percussive drum instruments.

Method: Fine-tuning a pre-trained text-to-audio model using a new dataset of paired drum audio with a content encoder and a hybrid conditioning mechanism.

Result: The model generates high-quality drum audio that aligns with high-resolution MIDI, showing strong performance in audio quality, rhythmic alignment, and beat continuity.

Conclusion: This approach provides producers with a controllable, efficient tool for drum loop creation, expanding the possibilities in digital music production.

Abstract: Current methods for creating drum loop audio in digital music production, such as using one-shot samples or resampling, often demand non-trivial efforts of creators. While recent generative models achieve high fidelity and adhere to text, they lack the specific control needed for such a task. Existing symbolic-to-audio research often focuses on single, tonal instruments, leaving the challenge of polyphonic, percussive drum synthesis unaddressed. We address this gap by introducing ``Break-the-Beat!,'' a model capable of rendering a drum MIDI with the timbre of a reference audio. It is built by fine-tuning a pre-trained text-to-audio model with our proposed content encoder and a effective hybrid conditioning mechanism. To enable this, we construct a new dataset of paired target-reference drum audio from existing drum audio datasets. Experiments demonstrate that our model generates high-quality drum audio that follows high-resolution drum MIDI, achieving strong performance across metrics of audio quality, rhythmic alignment, and beat continuity. This offer producers a new, controllable tool for creative production. Demo page: https://ik4sumii.github.io/break-the-beat/

</details>


<div id='math.NA'></div>

# math.NA [[Back]](#toc)

### [569] [Wavelet-Based Observables for Koopman Analysis: An Extended Dynamic Mode Decomposition Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14224)
*Cankat Tilki,Serkan Gugercin*

Main category: math.NA

TL;DR: This paper explores Koopman semigroup analysis using wavelet transforms and proposes the cWDMD algorithm, validated through numerical examples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding the Koopman semigroup's behavior and creating an efficient method for its numerical approximation.

Method: Introducing wavelet-based observables as eigenfunctions of the Koopman semigroup, constructing closed-form expressions, and proposing the cWDMD algorithm combining EDMD with wavelet-based observables.

Result: Theoretical results on Koopman semigroup expressions, validated via numerical testing of the cWDMD algorithm on two examples.

Conclusion: Wavelet-based observables and the cWDMD algorithm enhance Koopman semigroup analysis, offering a robust theoretical and numerical framework.

Abstract: We present an in-depth analysis of the Koopman semigroup via wavelet transform. Towards this goal, we start by introducing the wavelet-based observables and show that they are eigenfunctions of the Koopman semigroup when this semigroup is considered over the Banach space of continuous functions on a compact forward-invariant set endowed with the supremum norm. We then construct closed-form expressions of the action of the Koopman semigroup and its resolvent in terms of these observables. To approximate the action of Koopman semigroup numerically, we combine Extended Dynamic Mode Decomposition (EDMD) with the proposed wavelet-based observables leading to the Wavelet Dynamic Mode Decomposition via Continuous Wavelet Transform (cWDMD) algorithm. We validate our theoretical results on two numerical examples.

</details>


<div id='cs.GR'></div>

# cs.GR [[Back]](#toc)

### [570] [DiffPhD: A Unified Differentiable Solver for Projective Heterogeneous Materials in Elastodynamics with Contact-Rich GPU-Acceleration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14526)
*Shih-Yu Lai,Sung-Han Tien,Jui-I Huang,Yen-Chen Tseng,Yi-Ting Chiu,Siyuan Luo,Ziqiu Zeng,Fan Shi,Peter Yichen Chen,Tiantian Liu,Yu-Lun Liu,Bing-Yu Chen*

Main category: cs.GR

TL;DR: The paper introduces DiffPhD, a GPU-accelerated framework for differentiable Projective Dynamics capable of handling soft body simulations involving heterogeneous materials, hyperelasticity, and contact-rich interactions, achieving significant speed and accuracy improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The work aims to overcome limitations in existing methods like DiffPD which struggle with heterogeneous materials, extreme stiffness contrasts, hyperelasticity under large deformations, and contact-intensive scenarios common in realistic simulations.

Method: The authors integrate stiffness-aware projective weights, trust-region eigenvalue filtering, a type-II Anderson Acceleration scheme, and a unified GPU pipeline that reuses sparse factorization across computations. These allow for stable, efficient simulations and gradient calculations for challenging material configurations.

Result: DiffPhD provides up to an order-of-magnitude speedup compared to prior approaches, maintains stability with stiffness contrasts up to 100x, and enables gradient-based optimization for scenarios previously unachievable due to solver limitations.

Conclusion: DiffPhD demonstrates robust performance across challenging scenarios, offering precise gradients and improved efficiency, making it a powerful tool for realistic simulation tasks involving heterogeneous, hyperelastic, and contact-rich materials.

Abstract: Differentiable simulation of soft bodies is a foundation for system identification, trajectory optimization, and Real2Sim transfer. Yet, existing methods such as the differentiable Projective Dynamics (DiffPD) struggle when faced with heterogeneous materials with extreme stiffness contrasts, hyperelasticity under large deformations, and contact-rich interactions, which are common scenarios in the real world. We present DiffPhD, a unified GPU-accelerated differentiable Projective Dynamics framework for heterogeneous materials that tackles these intertwined challenges simultaneously. Our key insight is a careful integration of: (i) stiffness-aware projective weights to embed heterogeneity into the global system; (ii) trust-region eigenvalue filtering lifted to the backward pass for stable hyperelastic gradients and a type-II Anderson Acceleration scheme with dual-gate convergence to stabilize forward iteration under large stiffness contrasts; and (iii) a unified GPU pipeline that reuses a single sparse factor across forward, backward, and contact computations, with stiffness-amplified Rayleigh damping folded into the same factor for heterogeneity-aware dissipation at zero recurring cost. DiffPhD achieves strict gradient accuracy while delivering up to an order-of-magnitude speedup over prior differentiable solvers on heterogeneous, hyperelastic, contact-rich benchmarks. Crucially, this speedup does not come at the cost of stability: DiffPhD remains convergent on stiffness contrasts up to 100x where prior PD solvers degrade. This unlocks end-to-end gradient-based optimization on regimes previously bottlenecked by either solver fragility or per-iteration cost -- shell--joint composite creatures, soft characters wielding stiff weapons, and soft-gripper robotic manipulation -- all handled within a single forward--backward pass.

</details>


### [571] [FaceParts: Segmentation and Editing of Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13853)
*Tymoteusz Zapała,Julia Farganus,Dominik Galus,Mikołaj Czachorowski,Piotr Syga,Przemysław Spurek*

Main category: cs.GR

TL;DR: The paper introduces FaceParts, a 3D facial editing and segmentation framework that operates directly in the Gaussian space, achieving automated and semantically coherent part isolation without supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of automation and reliance on labor-intensive manual editing in 3D facial editing and segmentation, which is essential for industries like entertainment and virtual reality.

Method: FaceParts combines feature disentanglement, density-based clustering, and FLAME-anchored part transfer to segment and edit Gaussian Splatting avatars in an unsupervised manner.

Result: Experiments on the NeRSemble dataset show robust feature isolation (e.g., beards, eyebrows) and effective transfer across avatars, while maintaining high identity consistency (ID = 0.943), low Average Expression Distance (AED = 0.021), and low Average Pose Distance (APD = 0.004).

Conclusion: The proposed approach achieves precise and automated segmentation/editing of 3D avatars with high fidelity and potential applications in digital avatars and more.

Abstract: Facial editing is an important task with applications in entertainment, virtual reality, and digital avatars. Most existing approaches rely on generative models in the 2D image domain, while in 3D the task is typically performed through labor-intensive manual editing. We propose FaceParts, a framework for unsupervised segmentation and editing of Gaussian Splatting avatars. Unlike existing 2D or mesh-assisted methods, our approach operates directly in the Gaussian domain, decomposing avatars into semantically coherent facial parts without supervision. The method integrates feature disentanglement, density-based clustering, and FLAME-anchored part transfer, enabling precise editing and cross-avatar part swapping. Experiments on the NeRSemble dataset with 11 subjects demonstrate robust isolation of features such as beards, eyebrows, eyes and mustaches. Quantitative evaluation confirms that transferred segments adapt to pose and expression, while maintaining identity consistency (ID = 0.943), low Average Expression Distance (AED = 0.021) and low Average Pose Distance (APD = 0.004).

</details>


### [572] [SparseOIT: Improving Order-Independent Transparency 3DGS via Active Set Method](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13855)
*Wentao Yang,Fanzhen Kong,Zejian Kang,Xiangru Huang*

Main category: cs.GR

TL;DR: SparseOIT leverages sparsity in Order-Independent Transparency (OIT) modified equations for 3D Gaussian Splatting, achieving faster and competitive reconstruction results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations in 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) for rendering transparent or non-lambertian objects, where volumetric rendering struggles.

Method: The paper introduces SparseOIT, an optimization algorithm utilizing sparse variable dependencies from OIT-modified rendering equations to maintain an active set of Gaussian splats, thereby enhancing computational efficiency.

Result: SparseOIT significantly accelerates performance by exploiting sparsity and outperforms other methods in the OIT-family, while offering competitiveness with state-of-the-art volumetric rendering techniques.

Conclusion: Order-Independent Transparency (OIT) modifications enable sparse optimization techniques like SparseOIT, demonstrating improved efficiency and effectiveness in 3DGS reconstruction.

Abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has received tremendous popularity over the past few years due to its photorealistic visual appearance. However, 3DGS uses volumetric rendering that is not suitable for objects with non-lambertian or transparent materials. To remedy this issue, a family of Order-Independent Transparency (OIT) rendering methods propose to remove or modify the depth sorting step in the 3DGS rendering equation. However, the potential of OIT-based method is still underexplored. In this paper, we observe that the OIT modifications to the rendering equation significantly reduce the inter-independence among individual gaussian splats, resulting in very sparse variable dependencies that can be harnessed by specific optimization techniques such as active set method. To this end, we propose SparseOIT, an OIT-based 3DGS reconstruction algorithm that maintains an active set of gaussian splats and enjoys an acceleration ratio that is proportional to the potential sparsity. SparseOIT is designed by jointly considering the OIT rendering equation, the reconstruction algorithm and the geometric regularization. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that SparseOIT outperforms existing methods in the OIT-family by a large margin and also achieves comparable performance to the state-of-the-art 3DGS reconstruction methods based on volumetric rendering. Project page:

</details>


### [573] [Realiz3D: 3D Generation Made Photorealistic via Domain-Aware Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13852)
*Ido Sobol,Kihyuk Sohn,Yoav Blum,Egor Zakharov,Max Bluvstein,Andrea Vedaldi,Or Litany*

Main category: cs.GR

TL;DR: Realiz3D is a new framework designed to generate realistic and 3D-consistent images by decoupling visual domain from control signals, mitigating the realism loss often caused by using synthetic 3D asset renders.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to overcome the domain gap between photorealistic images and synthetic renders, which hampers realistic image generation with precise controls.

Method: The framework introduces a co-variate fed into residual adapters to separately learn visual domains (real or synthetic) and applies novel training/inference strategies based on characteristics of diffusion-based generators.

Result: Realiz3D enables photorealistic and 3D-consistent multi-view image generation and texturing tasks with controllability and reduced domain gap issues.

Conclusion: By separating visual domains from control signals and optimizing training strategies, Realiz3D effectively generates realistic images while maintaining precise geometry and material controls.

Abstract: We often aim to generate images that are both photorealistic and 3D-consistent, adhering to precise geometry, material, and viewpoint controls. Typically, this is achieved by fine-tuning an image generator, pre-trained on billions of real images, using renders of synthetic 3D assets, where annotations for control signals are available. While this approach can learn the desired controls, it often compromises the realism of the images due to domain gap between photographs and renders. We observe that this issue largely arises from the model learning an unintended association between the presence of control signals and the synthetic appearance of the images. To address this, we introduce Realiz3D, a lightweight framework for training diffusion models, that decouples controls and visual domain. The key idea is to explicitly learn visual domain, real or synthetic, separately from other control signals by introducing a co-variate that, fed into small residual adapters, shifts the domain. Then, the generator can be trained to gain controllability, without fitting to specific visual domain. In this way, the model can be guided to produce realistic images even when controls are applied. We enhance control transferability to the real domain by leveraging insights about roles of different layers and denoising steps in diffusion-based generators, informing new training and inference strategies that further mitigate the gap. We demonstrate the advantages of Realiz3D in tasks as text-to-multiview generation and texturing from 3D inputs, producing outputs that are 3D-consistent and photorealistic.

</details>


### [574] [MoZoo:Unleashing Video Diffusion power in animal fur and muscle simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13857)
*Dongxia Liu,Jie Ma,Xiaochen Yang,Jiancheng Zhang,Bin Xia,Zhehan Kan,Nisha Huang,Jun Liang,Wenming Yang,Jin Li*

Main category: cs.GR

TL;DR: The paper introduces MoZoo, a generative dynamics solver, to create realistic animal videos from coarse meshes using advanced techniques like RAR-RoPE and Asymmetric Decoupled Attention, supported by synthetic and benchmark datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of labor-intensive and computationally costly traditional workflows for modeling muscle and fur dynamics in creating cinematic-quality animal effects.

Method: It proposes MoZoo, enhanced with techniques such as RAR-RoPE for motion alignment, Asymmetric Decoupled Attention for information flow optimization, and MoZoo-Data, a synthetic-to-real pipeline for dataset creation, along with MoZooBench for evaluation.

Result: MoZoo demonstrated high-fidelity, temporally and structurally consistent fur simulation across various animal skeletons and layouts, validated by experimental results.

Conclusion: The proposed methods enable efficient and realistic simulation of animal dynamics, making high-quality effects more accessible while advancing generative diffusion modeling capabilities.

Abstract: The creation of cinematic-quality animal effects necessitates the precise modeling of muscle and fur dynamics, a process that remains both labor-intensive and computationally expensive within traditional production workflows. While generative diffusion models have shown promise in diverse artistic workflows, their capacity for high-fidelity animal simulation remains largely unexploited. We present MoZoo, a generative dynamics solver that bypasses conventional refinement to synthesize high-fidelity animal videos from coarse meshes under multimodal guidance. We propose Role-Aware RoPE (RAR-RoPE) which employs role-based index remapping to synchronize motion alignment while decoupling reference information via fixed temporal offsets. Complementing this, Asymmetric Decoupled Attention partitions the latent sequence to enforce a unidirectional information flow, effectively preventing feature interference and improving computational efficiency. To address the scarcity of high-quality training data, we introduce MoZoo-Data, a synthetic-to-real pipeline that leverages a rendering engine and an inverse mapping approach to construct a large-scale dataset of paired sequences. Furthermore, we establish MoZooBench, a comprehensive benchmark with 120 mesh-video pairs. Experimental results demonstrate that MoZoo achieves high-fidelity fur simulation across diverse animal skeletons and layouts, preserving superior temporal and structural consistency.

</details>


### [575] [Seed3D 2.0: Advancing High-Fidelity Simulation-Ready 3D Content Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13862)
*Diandian Gu,Jing Lin,Gaohong Liu,Jiahang Liu,Su Ma,Guang Shi,Jun Wang,Qinlong Wang,Qianyi Wu,Zhongcong Xu,Xuanyu Yi,Zihao Yu,Jianfeng Zhang,Zhuolin Zheng,Yifan Zhu,Rui Chen,Hengkai Guo,Xiaoyang Guo,Mingcong Han,Xu Han,Xiu Li,Yixun Liang,Weiqiang Lou,Junzhe Lu,Guan Luo,Minghan Qin,Shuguang Wang,Yuang Wang*

Main category: cs.GR

TL;DR: Seed3D 2.0 is a significant update to the 3D content generation platform that enhances geometry, textures, and application scope, achieving superior performance compared to commercial alternatives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to advance the fidelity, functionality, and application scope of 3D content generation to meet the increasing demand for high-quality and simulation-ready digital assets.

Method: The system employs a coarse-to-fine geometry pipeline, a locality-aware VAE for spatial efficiency, a unified PBR model for texture generation, and introduces a simulation-ready model suite for coherent scene construction and physics-based interactions.

Result: Seed3D 2.0 demonstrated significant gains, achieving user preference win rates of 69.0% to 89.9% over five leading commercial models in textured 3D asset generation.

Conclusion: The improvements in Seed3D 2.0 position it as a superior tool for generating textured and simulation-ready 3D assets, enabling broader applicability in computer graphics and physics-based simulations.

Abstract: We present Seed3D 2.0, an advanced 3D content generation system built on Seed3D 1.0, with substantial improvements across generation fidelity, simulation-ready capabilities, and application coverage. For geometry, a coarse-to-fine two-stage pipeline decouples global structure learning from high-frequency detail recovery, while a locality-aware VAE achieves higher spatial compression and more efficient decoding. For texture and material generation, we replace the cascaded pipeline of Seed3D 1.0 with a unified PBR model that directly generates multi-view albedo and metallic-roughness maps, enhanced by Mixture-of-Experts scaling and VLM-based semantic conditioning for improved material precision and visual fidelity. Beyond single-object generation, Seed3D 2.0 introduces a simulation-ready model suite comprising scene layout planning, part-aware decomposition, and training-free articulation generation, enabling coherent scene construction and part-level physical interaction across physics and graphics engines. A large-scale human preference study against five recent commercial models shows that Seed3D 2.0 achieves consistent win rates of 69.0% to 89.9% in textured 3D asset generation. Seed3D 2.0 is available on https://exp.volcengine.com/ark/vision?_vtm_=0.0.c70961.d701978.0&mode=vision&modelId=doubao-seed3d-2-0-260328&tab=Gen3D

</details>


### [576] [AnchorRoute: Human Motion Synthesis with Interval-Routed Sparse Contro](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14716)
*Pengcheng Fang,Tengjiao Sun,Dongjie Fu,Xiaoyu Zhan,Yanwen Guo,Hansung Kim,Xiaohao Cai*

Main category: cs.GR

TL;DR: AnchorRoute is a motion synthesis framework that uses sparse anchors to generate and refine detailed human motion, improving adherence to sparse control in evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper focuses on addressing the challenge of generating full-body human motion from sparse control points (anchors), enabling efficient motion authoring while maintaining generation quality.

Method: The framework uses a pretrained text-to-motion diffusion prior, with anchor-conditioned features for generation, and a refinement step using RouteSolver to improve adherence to the anchors.

Result: AnchorRoute outperforms previous sparse-control methods in benchmark evaluations by achieving better alignment with sparse anchors and maintaining text-motion quality.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates that combining anchor-conditioned generation with residual-routed refinement enhances both motion quality and control specificity, making it a robust solution for human motion synthesis.

Abstract: Sparse anchors provide a compact interface for human motion authoring: users specify a few root positions, planar trajectory samples, or body-point targets, while the system synthesizes the full-body motion that completes the under-specified intent. We present AnchorRoute, a sparse-anchor motion synthesis framework that uses anchors as a shared scaffold for both generation and refinement. Before generation, AnchorRoute converts sparse anchors into anchor-condition features and injects the resulting condition memory into a frozen Transition Masked Diffusion prior through AnchorKV and dual-context conditioning. This preserves the generation quality of the pretrained text-to-motion prior while learning sparse spatial control. After generation, the same anchors are evaluated as residuals: their timestamps define refinement intervals, and their residuals determine where correction should be concentrated. RouteSolver then refines the motion by projecting soft-token updates onto anchor-defined piecewise-affine interval bases. This couples generation-time anchor conditioning with residual-routed refinement under one anchor scaffold. AnchorRoute supports root-3D, planar-root, and body-point control within the same formulation. In benchmark evaluations, AnchorRoute outperforms prior sparse-control methods under the sparse keyjoint protocol and consistently improves anchor adherence across control families. The results show that the learned anchor-conditioned generator and RouteSolver refinement are complementary: the generator preserves text-motion quality, while RouteSolver provides a controllable path toward stronger anchor adherence.

</details>


### [577] [UMo: Unified Sparse Motion Modeling for Real-Time Co-Speech Avatars](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14731)
*Xiaoyu Zhan,Xinyu Fu,Chenghao Yang,Xiaohong Zhang,Dongjie Fu,Pengcheng Fang,Tengjiao Sun,Xiaohao Cai,Hansung Kim,Yuanqi Li,Jie Guo,Yanwen Guo*

Main category: cs.GR

TL;DR: The paper proposes UMo, a model for real-time co-speech avatars, which incorporates text, audio, and motion data for high-fidelity animations, achieving better results than existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome limitations in existing methods that fail to fully utilize multimodal data or suffer from constrained model representation and real-time performance in generating expressive digital avatars.

Method: UMo leverages a unified sparse motion architecture, combining a spatially sparse Mixture-of-Experts framework and a temporally sparse, keyframe-centric design. It also introduces a multi-stage training strategy with targeted audio augmentation.

Result: UMo produces temporally coherent, high-fidelity animations for facial and gesture expressions, delivering low-latency performance and better speech-motion alignment under real-time constraints.

Conclusion: The proposed UMo architecture offers a practical solution for creating high-quality, real-time co-speech avatars that outperform existing methods in both quality and latency.

Abstract: Speech-driven gestures and facial animations are fundamental to expressive digital avatars in games, virtual production, and interactive media.
